meta.scene_summary: The industry is currently in a race to see if we can ship products faster than the subpoenas can arrive.

cooking.headline: Anthropic adds a former Fed Chair to their board because AI needs more interest rate anxiety.
cooking.summary: Anthropic recruited Ben Bernanke to help oversee their AI safety, which is a massive pivot from managing global financial recessions to managing LLM hallucinations. It's comforting to know that if the AI crashes the economy, we'll at least have the guy who wrote the book on how to fix it.
cooking.plain_english: Former Federal Reserve Chairman Ben Bernanke has joined the board of directors at Anthropic to assist with the company's long-term safety and governance strategy.

cooking.headline: Police just performed the ultimate git reset on 5,800 global scammers.
cooking.summary: Law enforcement agencies across the globe just coordinated a massive takedown, proving that even international fraud rings aren't immune to a sudden, forced deployment of justice. It's the closest thing to a global 'ban user' command that the internet has seen all year.
cooking.plain_english: International law enforcement agencies arrested 5,800 suspects and seized significant assets as part of a coordinated global operation against online fraud.

cooking.headline: GitHub finally put a landlord in charge of every repository
cooking.summary: GitHub is forcing every repo to have a durable owner, effectively ending the era of 'orphan' code that nobody wants to claim. It's like finally assigning a point person for that one group project file that everyone has been afraid to touch for three years.
cooking.plain_english: GitHub has implemented a new system to ensure every repository is assigned a permanent, accountable owner to improve security and maintenance.

cooking.headline: European unions want a 'server overheat' protocol for human employees.
cooking.summary: Large unions are demanding the right to stop working when temperatures hit 30C, which sounds suspiciously like thermal throttling for people. If your office doesn't have better cooling than a rack of servers, it turns out you might be legally entitled to go home.
cooking.plain_english: Major labor unions in Europe are requesting that employees be allowed to stop working on days when the temperature exceeds 30 degrees Celsius.

cooked.headline: OpenAI claims they can't search their own logs, which is a bold strategy in a copyright trial
cooked.summary: OpenAI told the court their training data logs were essentially lost in the void, despite being the same people who built the world's most advanced search tools. It's like a librarian claiming they've never seen a book while standing in the middle of the library.
cooked.plain_english: The New York Times alleges that OpenAI falsely claimed it could not search its internal records during a legal dispute over copyright infringement.

cooked.headline: OpenAI is playing hide-and-seek with evidence, but the court is not having it
cooked.summary: Trying to hide billions of logs during a lawsuit is like trying to hide a massive technical debt folder during a code review. You might think you've tucked it away, but eventually, someone is going to run a recursive search and find everything.
cooked.plain_english: OpenAI is accused of withholding evidence that could prove how it trained its AI models during an ongoing copyright lawsuit.

cooked.headline: China is trying to put a firewall around their open-source AI models
cooked.summary: Building open-source AI is supposed to be about global collaboration, but China is now considering restricting access to their models for foreign users. It turns out the 'open' in open-source is becoming more of a suggestion than a promise.
cooked.plain_english: The Chinese government may implement new rules to prevent users outside of China from accessing and using Chinese artificial intelligence models.

cooked.headline: The EU just told Meta that infinite scrolling is officially bad for your health
cooked.summary: Meta spent years engineering an algorithm designed to keep you glued to your phone, and now the EU is pointing out that the feature is actually a design flaw. Being legally required to make your product less addictive is the ultimate 'it's not you, it's me' breakup.
cooked.plain_english: The European Commission has determined that the design of Instagram and Facebook encourages addictive behavior, violating the Digital Services Act.

cooked.headline: China's public QR codes are turning into digital landmines
cooked.summary: Public infrastructure has become a game of Russian roulette where scanning a menu might actually launch a browser full of scams. It turns out that trusting a paper sticker in a public space is the physical equivalent of clicking a 'you won an iPad' pop-up in 2005.
cooked.plain_english: Criminals are replacing legitimate public QR codes in China with malicious ones that redirect users to scam or adult websites.

cooked.headline: The CFO speedran a $67M money laundering scheme instead of optimizing the backend.
cooked.summary: The Epoch Times CFO just pleaded guilty to laundering $67 million, proving that some people treat corporate finance like a high-stakes capture the flag event. It turns out that 'creative accounting' is just a fancy way to land yourself in a federal prison cell.
cooked.plain_english: The former Chief Financial Officer of The Epoch Times has admitted to participating in a money laundering scheme involving $67 million.
